sentences.text: Yes.
And I-- in the middle of there where I jumped, next to me was a man shot right in his-- in his hand.
And the blood was running from his hand.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I felt terrible because that was so much blood that man lost.
He took me and him in his house.
He make me a bandage.
And, and he took me to a lady in Zolkiew.
I jumped in Zolkiew.
And I was crying all my heart out, where my sister is.
Because of her, I went back to ghetto.
I didn't had to go back any more, if she would be there where I was.
Only because I went to pick her up, I got caught.
And I took me in here, I haven't got my sister.
And the Jewish-- the Jewish family took me in.
And they start to ask me all sorts of question.
And I had a rest with the daughter.
She had-- she was sick, and I was lying there.
In the morning, a lady came and said that a little-- they picked up dead people, and a little child was taken with them.
And she was trying to make that she is not alive, because she couldn't recognize Jewish and German.
So she was lying like that, only they felt that she is alive.
And they did shot her on the leg.
And the bull went in and out.
And she had maybe 10 holes.
And it was so smelly.
Anyway, I find out that my sister is alive.
And I was running with that lady to the committee-- committees, they had some beds for sick people, for old people.
And there was my little sister.
And I took her with me.
And I asked where is a doctor.
And I had earrings, little earrings from home.
And I give to the doctor.
And he gave us some special tablets for anti-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to infection, not to get infected.
Only she was, I think, terrible.
And then I went back to that committee.
And I looked for the president of it, to ask him a favor, if he could arrange for me somebody to get me back to Lemberg, because I still got a father and a brother.
And I knew exactly where they are.
He said, yes he will arrange.
And he came next day.
And he bought tickets for us to the train, and he put us to the train.
He gave me-- he told me that he wants to take me also away.
Next day, he will bring me a nice fur, and shoes.
He wants to save my life.
And I was very happy.
No.
And I was very happy that he offered me such a good thing.
And when I went back, we was on the train.
Everyone could see that we Jewish.
No one said a word.
My little sister couldn't walk, I had to carry her on my back.
Then from the train, we had to go to a tram.
Because the train is all different.
And I am on a tram.
Everyone sees us, two little girls sitting.
And I knew the stop.
We went there, and I took her on my back.
And I heard, on the way, marching Jewish people to work, and the German who used to scream, left, [GERMAN], right.
He took them working.
And I was frightened he will not see us.
And I quick went into that big door, and I start to bang.
And they opened for me the door.
And the monk said-- start to scream, Mr.
Heller, Heller.
Your daughters are here.
And everybody was hidden, because it was night.
Maybe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 6:00, I don't remember the time.
Only now it was very dark.
[NON-ENGLISH], yeah, [NON-ENGLISH] Street in Lemberg.
And my father stopped talking for happiness.
For a few minutes, he lost his voice.
He was so happy.
And my brother, and another family.
There was few families there, Jewish families.
And my sister was taken straight away to hospital.
The monk took her at the hospital.
And I had to be still there, to study the, the Catechism, to know the praying, the everyday praying you must know.
So I had to learn.
When you're young, you study quick.
So I, I did study it, in the cellar, where I told you was very big rats.
I was very unpleasant to be there.
And one day, in the daytime, they said they got a little storage room.
They will put me there.
It will be better for me.
And they built a big-- like for a dog, a kennel, a big kennel.
And on top, they put little ladder.
And I was sitting there.
I had a bit of fresh air.
Only the director had a message, a telephone message that inspectors are going to come, to watch every spot, if they didn't have hidden some ladder.
So the director comes into that little storage room and sees such a big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thing lying.
He didn't know what it is.
So he start-- he called somebody to take it away.
And he opened, and I am there.
He start to scream that he have to give me away.
If I don't disappear, he will notified the Germans and this and that.
Then was a holiday, Easter holiday.
And Lazar-- his name was Lazar-- monk, one monk, a small one.
He took me to the-- he bought me a beautiful-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] something on my head to wear and a nice skirt.
And he took me to the church.
And how I go into the church, straight away they gave me to carry a little flag, like Maria, Santa Maria with Jesus.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very happy.
And then he said to me, you know what?
I will go-- I will take you to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is the lady, the nun, nun, which she is a superior.
She looks after everybody.
That was a Ukrainian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] convent.
And how he introduced me to her, she likes me.
She said, OK, she is good.
And she took me in.
And she wants to settle me with the girls, Ukrainian girls.
And I was very worried about.
Because my Ukrainian language wasn't so good.
And I was frightened that I will recognize straight away that I am not a Ukrainian.
So I wrote her a letter that I would like her to take me in her candidate to be a nun.
I feel that I will be suitable to look after-- to give my life away for Jesus.
And they agreed.
They give me-- they send me to Jaktorow, their little town.
And there I was with the nuns.
I didn't have to talk.
It was, for me, a relaxation.
No, not the nuns.
Nobody knew that I am Jewish.
And there, I have been working in all sorts of positions.
Once I were in the kitchen.
I am a left-hander, so it's-- wasn't so good for me.
I couldn't peel potatoes well-- very thick and every little was sorry to waste.
And for left-handed, it wasn't such a good job.
So I asked for a different job.
I was sawing the wood.
I like to work more men jobs.
They didn't care.
They sent me to look after the pigs and after the little chickens.
And I was with the nuns.
And there, I felt so good.
At least I was a bit relaxed.
One day, I-- we had maybe 40 nuns, young nuns and a few, a few candidates to be nuns.
You have to be very careful.
You have to behave well.
And one day, I couldn't stop.
I was laughing without stop.
It probably were nerves.
I couldn't stop laughing.
Only the nuns couldn't-- they saw I am a child.
Only I got a punishment that I cannot finish my food.
Not because I was Jewish.
That was their way of treating everybody.
And I had to carry everywhere my plate.
And I didn't mind.
I took it very good.
And I had to tell to everyone, in front of every nun, that I was not behaving well, and I am sorry.
And the punishment was not so pleasant.
Only I didn't care, I was young.
For me, it was nothing.
I had to kiss everyone on the legs.
Every nun would pass the corridor to meals, I had to kneel and kiss them on the legs.
That was the punishment.
And And for a few days, I had to carry everywhere my little plate.
I didn't mind.
And I passed it very well.
And then, they-- we had every [INAUDIBLE] and there was a special break to speak about the other world, what will happen.
Saint Peter and he's there, and he will open the door.
And they will pray to me, I will take them in.
They believe in me that I will be a saint.
And really, I promise myself that if the war will be finished, and I will not find anyone from my family, I'm not going to marry.
I will become a nun.
That was my wish, because I wouldn't marry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I want to be a nun.
Only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's what happened.
And I, I prayed every morning. 5:00 in the morning, we went to the [NON-ENGLISH], and we were doing our praying.
And then, every Sunday comes the priest, and we had to tell him what we'd done wrong.
Even a dream, a bad dream, you had to tell, too.
And I took it very nice.
And I studied to, to learn the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Catechism, the Bible.
And I loved it.
I think it's good.
I was sorry why we couldn't do that and not to suffer so much.
Because the religion is the one bad thing what makes the problem.
We all decent people, and we suffer.
And I don't know why.
Even Jesus was Jewish, and now everybody prays to him.
And we, the children of-- of his nation, suffering.
This is not right.
Anyway, that's what-- what shall I tell you?
It was very hard.
Only for me, it was easier.
One day, I felt very sick.
I had an abscess under the arm.
And I was really thinking I'm going to die.
So they ask me what I wish to do, before I die.
I said, I have a little sister.
Her name is [?
Olania ?] [?
Hanoshac ?].
We had, we have been there not as sisters.
I, my name was Wolodimira Billa, and her name was [?
Olania ?] [?
Hanoshac ?].
And I was in a little town called Jaktorow, and we wasn't-- we was not supposed to tell anybody that we're sisters.
Only before I die-- before my father let me in to the-- because that's all my father arranged.
He told me, remember, you're a Jewish girl, and you're older than [NON-ENGLISH].
You must make sure that when the war finish, you have to be Jewish, because you are born Jewish.
And this-- Daddy's words were very important for me, [SOBS] because I loved my family very much.
Every word, I try to remember.
And I wish to-- I told the nun that I'd like to see my sister.
She is in Lemberg.
Her name is [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH].
Before I die, I want her to see me.
And they did send her to me.
And I told her, and she was very, very religious.
She said she will never be Jewish. because Jewish people got first big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], and this, to be Catholic, is much better.
And she doesn't want to know about-- anyway, I told her, remember, that's what Daddy wants you to be.
If I be not alive, you must remember that you become-- you come from a Jewish family and try to keep it going.
And they send me a priest.
And I, I had a [NON-ENGLISH], this is special beads.
You go around, and you pray every day with it.
And you pray to God, to let you stay alive and to do what he feel to do shall be done.
And I was ready to die.
Only, that wound opened, and I was alive.
And I start again to be with them.
I appreciate what they were doing for me.
And I was really trying to do everything correct for the nuns.
And I loved them.
They were very nice to me.
There was a little girl of Rabbi Kahane.
His daughter was with me and his-- and his wife.
Yeah.
He was a rabbi.
Before the war, he comes from Lemberg.
And he was in Israel.
He gave a few marriages to my friends, girlfriends, which marry in Israel.
And he promised that after the war he will, he will try to keep all our Jewish children to be Catholic.
And he will be-- he will support Catholicism.
Only you know, when you want to leave, you have to tell all sorts of things.
Because everybody sticks to his belief.
And that little girl used to make wee-wee in her bed.
Her name was Roma.
Every day they put the wee-wee on her-- the sheet on her head.
Not because she is Jewish, just that is the way punishment was.
And there were a few Jewish children.
Only I-- she was in my, in Jaktorow, where I was.
And my sister was there, too, and then they took her away to another place, because we was not allowed to be together.
There was also a bakery business, German bakery business.
We was very afraid that they don't recognize us, me.
So I was working in the pig sty.
And the pigs were so big, I was so frightened for them.
And smelly, I felt that I can't take it and I couldn't help.
In the night we used to work in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where grows apples.
And we work there a lot of trees.
And we had to watch, watch at night, we had to watch so nobody comes and pinch it.
So I was watching with a nun.
All night, we were there, and she let me have an apple.
Because we was not allowed to eat.
Only because I was young, they gave me an apple.
And I had a lovely nun.
She used to call me Wolodia.
My name was Wolodia-- Wolodimira, Wolodia, they-- Wolodia, every four hours I ate.
And she looked after me.
She was so nice.
One day, somebody came and tell me that my daddy and my brother was killed.
And I start crying very much, because I miss-- I thought I will see my daddy.
And now I know he's not alive any more.
So I went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
This is a little church, domestic one.
And I start to cry very strongly.
And they call me out.
The [NON-ENGLISH] asked, why you crying?
So I told her that something else.
I didn't want to tell her that; I told her that I am sorry that Jesus was killed, something different.
And I didn't tell her that that's the reason.
Because I was frightened.
Who came to tell you that your father and brother were killed?
Who knew that you were Jewish?
Somebody, the monk come to visit me one day.
And he told me that.
The monk.
Yeah, I know.
They took him away from that place where he was.
Because they was frightened too many Jews working.
So they took him to Janowska-- in Janowska was a camp-- with my brother.
He had to warm up his hands, because he had to cut a few bags from leather.
And when you haven't got warm hands, you cannot cut.
And when the German saw him warming his hands, he start to belt him with his gun.
And my brother went on the back, to choke the German.
So straight away he shot my father and my brother.
That's what I got, the news.
So I wasn't happy about.
And I, I couldn't stop crying.
I was, all the time, very upset.
Only with kids, you know, comes few days.
You, you forget.
You start to-- you remember, only you cannot live all the time with this.
And you take yourself in hand.
And you carry on your life.
Because you can get cuckoo, if you go back all the time and think of things what happened.
You cannot bring them back, and you have to think healthy.
And we went through a lot.
And we-- after the war, the Russian-- I was in the convent.
And the nuns knew that the Russians are very-- they like ladies.
They took us in the cellar to make butter, and put this big-- on the head, to cover the faces, not to see our faces.
Only they came after, and they said all young children must go to school.
So they send us to orphanage.
And I was sent after the war, in a orphan in Lemberg, called [NON-ENGLISH].
From there, I used to go to school.
Only I still was Ukrainian.
I was [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH].
After the war, I didn't change my name.
And [NON-ENGLISH], my sister, was still in the-- with the nuns.
And she didn't want to come with me.
And the nuns kept her.
Because there was a young school at nuns, they could keep her.
Only I did want her to come with me.
So one day, I travel up where she was--
After the war.
And I said, [NON-ENGLISH], you have to come with me.
She said-- oh, no.
I come up, and I asked a nun that I want to talk to my sister.
And they said, she's very sick.
You cannot see her.
So I went back, you know.
And then I met-- No, I didn't tell you everything.
After the war, they told me-- the nuns-- that I can go and have a look in the Jewish committee if somebody is alive from my family.
I went there.
I had different clothing.
I had from, from sack such a dress.
I wasn't dressed as a nun.
Just I had a special white thing.
And I went with a normal [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] scarf.
I wore a scarf.
And I went to look on the list.
And then I notice a lady from my back yard.
[NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] was her name.
I still couldn't find her.
And that was in Lemberg.
And I said-- and she said to me, where are you?
I said, I cannot tell you.
Because I still had to go back with the nun.
The nun was waiting for me outside.
And I didn't tell.
And she give me the address where she is.
And I said to her that I will be in a orphan.
Somehow, I contacted her.
I got her address, I don't know, I gave her-- and she-- when I was in the orphanage, she came to visit me.
And she said, where is your sister?
I said, she is with the nuns.
So why don't you bring her?
She doesn't want to come.
So she gave me the idea to tell that her auntie is alive, and she's also Ukrainian.
She lost her children, so she will come with me.
So I went back.
And I said to the nun that I would like to see my sister.
She said, she cannot see you.
I said, yes, she must see me.
And she let her out.
And she spoke to me.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH], you have to come with me.
We've got an auntie, which she lost her children.
And she wants us to adopt, we will be with her.
And she went back to the-- and I said, if you don't come, I will go to the police.
And I'll tell that they holding you, they don't want to let you out.
She went to the nun.
She told her that.
And one nun comes back to me.
She said, you can be ashamed.
Because we did for you so much.
And you're going to tell on us.
And I said, no, I don't do anything against you.
I just want my sister.
We have an auntie, which lost her children.
And she want us to be with her.
So that's all, let her go.
And she let her go.
And we-- I took her to the nuns, to the orphanage.
And there we were together.
It was not bad.
We went to school.
We-- we had what everybody had.
No mother, no father, just kids.
We were together.
